Jupiter King of the Gods.title: Roman Religion: How It Differed from Greek
content: The Romans borrowed heavily from Greek mythology, but they weren't just copying. They renamed the gods (Zeus became Jupiter, Athena became Minerva) and gave them different personalities that reflected Roman values.

Roman religion was practical and focused on maintaining pax deorum - 'peace with the gods.' Romans believed that if they performed rituals correctly, the gods would protect Rome. Religion was almost like a contract: we give offerings and follow the rules, you keep Rome safe.

Unlike Greeks who told stories of gods behaving badly, Romans emphasized duty, honor, and service to the state. Their gods were more dignified and focused on protecting Rome itself rather than having wild adventures.

Every Roman household had a lararium - a small shrine to household gods (Lares and Penates). Romans made daily offerings of food, wine, and incense. Religion was woven into everyday life, not just special occasions.
Jupiter King of the Gods.lesson: Roman mythology shows us what Romans valued: duty, order, military strength, and loyalty to the state. Their gods reflected the culture of an empire that conquered most of the known world through discipline and organization.

The Founding of Rome.title: Archaeological Evidence for Ancient Rome
content: The legend says Rome was founded in 753 BC by Romulus. Archaeologists have found evidence of settlements on the Palatine Hill dating to around 800-750 BC - close to the legendary date!
The seven hills of Rome weren't just myth. Modern Rome still sits on those hills: Palatine, Aventine, Capitoline, Quirinal, Viminal, Esquiline, and Caelian. You can visit them today.

Excavations have uncovered the remains of the earliest Roman Forum, dating to around the 7th century BC. Stone foundations, pottery, and burial sites confirm that Rome really did start as a small village of shepherds and farmers.

The Romulus and Remus story (raised by a wolf) is legend, but it reflects a real historical pattern: Rome was founded by Latin and Sabine peoples coming together. The myth of Romulus kidnapping Sabine women represents the historical merger of two cultures.
The Founding of Rome.lesson: Rome's foundation myths contain kernels of historical truth. While we can't prove Romulus existed, archaeology confirms that Rome began as a small settlement around 750 BC and grew into one of history's greatest empires.

Romulus and Remus.title: The She-Wolf Symbol of Rome
content: The Capitoline Wolf statue in Rome (showing a wolf nursing Romulus and Remus) is one of the most famous symbols of the city. The wolf part dates to ancient times, though the twins were added during the Renaissance.
Wolves were sacred to Mars, the god of war and father of Romulus. Romans saw wolves as symbols of strength, courage, and survival - perfect for a city built by warriors.
The phrase 'raised by wolves' comes from this story and means someone who grew up tough, wild, and able to survive anything. It became a metaphor for Roman toughness and military prowess.

Archaeological evidence shows that Romans really did revere wolves. Wolf imagery appears on coins, armor, shields, and monuments throughout Roman history. The founding myth shaped Roman identity for over 1,000 years.
Romulus and Remus.lesson: Foundation myths matter because they tell us what a culture values. Rome chose a story about abandoned twins raised by a wolf and founded through violence - this reflects Roman values of toughness, resilience, and military strength.
